# Useful Tools for Spatial Planning {#sec-useful-tools}

This chapter provides an overview of R packages that are useful for building *shinyplanr* applications and conducting spatial prioritisation analyses. We cover data acquisition with *oceandatr*, planning workflows with *spatialplanr*, and optimisation with *prioritizr*.

## Overview

The *shinyplanr* ecosystem relies on several interconnected packages:

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[oceandatr] -->|Data| B[spatialgridr]
    B -->|Grid| C[spatialplanr]
    C -->|Climate-smart<br>features| D[prioritizr]
    D -->|Solutions| E[shinyplanr]
```

| Package | Purpose | Website |
|---------|---------|---------|
| *oceandatr* | Download marine spatial data | [emlab-ucsb.github.io/oceandatr](https://emlab-ucsb.github.io/oceandatr/) |
| *spatialgridr* | Create and manipulate spatial grids | [emlab-ucsb.github.io/spatialgridr](https://emlab-ucsb.github.io/spatialgridr/) |
| *spatialplanr* | Climate-smart planning utilities | [spatialplanning.github.io/spatialplanr](https://spatialplanning.github.io/spatialplanr/) |
| *prioritizr* | Conservation prioritisation | [prioritizr.net](https://prioritizr.net/) |

## oceandatr {#sec-oceandatr}

The *oceandatr* package provides simple functions for acquiring, processing, and gridding ocean data. It is particularly useful for creating initial data layers when setting up *shinyplanr* for a new region.

### Installation

```r
if (!require(remotes)) install.packages("remotes")
options(timeout = 9999)  # Large package, may need extended timeout
remotes::install_github("emlab-ucsb/oceandatr")
```

### Getting Boundaries

Use `get_boundary()` to obtain EEZ or land boundaries:

```r
library(oceandatr)

# Get Exclusive Economic Zone
fiji_eez <- get_boundary(name = "Fiji", type = "eez")

# Get land boundary
fiji_land <- get_boundary(name = "Fiji", type = "land")

# Plot to verify
plot(fiji_eez[1], col = "lightblue")
```

[IMAGE: Example of EEZ boundary plot for a Pacific island nation]

::: {.callout-tip}
## Finding Country Names
Country names must match the [Marine Regions](https://www.marineregions.org/gazetteer.php) database. Search there if your country name is not recognised.
:::

### Creating Spatial Grids

Use `get_grid()` to create planning unit grids:

```r
# Define projection (equal-area for spatial planning)
fiji_projection <- "+proj=cea +lon_0=178 +lat_ts=-17 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs"

# Create hexagonal grid
fiji_grid <- get_grid(
  boundary = fiji_eez,
  resolution = 10000,  # 10 km resolution
  crs = fiji_projection
)

# Plot the grid
terra::plot(fiji_grid, col = "gold3", legend = FALSE)
```

Grid output options:

- `"terra"`: SpatRaster object (default)
- `"sf_hex"`: Hexagonal sf polygons
- `"sf_square"`: Square sf polygons

### Bathymetry

Download bathymetry data from GEBCO:

```r
# Raw bathymetry (depth values)
bathymetry <- get_bathymetry(
  spatial_grid = fiji_grid,
  classify_bathymetry = FALSE
)

# Classified depth zones
depth_zones <- get_bathymetry(
  spatial_grid = fiji_grid,
  classify_bathymetry = TRUE
)
```

Classified depth zones include:

| Zone | Depth Range |
|------|-------------|
| Continental Shelf | 0 - 200 m |
| Upper Bathyal | 200 - 800 m |
| Lower Bathyal | 800 - 3,500 m |
| Abyssal | 3,500 - 6,500 m |
| Hadal | > 6,500 m |

[IMAGE: Example bathymetry map with depth zones]

### Geomorphology

Download seafloor geomorphological features:

```r
geomorphology <- get_geomorphology(spatial_grid = fiji_grid)

# Remove empty layers (features not present in your area)
geomorphology <- remove_empty_layers(geomorphology)
```

Features include ridges, canyons, plateaus, escarpments, trenches, and more (from Harris et al. 2014).

### Seamounts and Knolls

```r
# Seamounts: peaks > 1000m above seafloor
seamounts <- get_seamounts(
  spatial_grid = fiji_grid,
  buffer = 30000  # 30 km buffer around peaks
)

# Knolls: smaller features (200-1000m)
knolls <- get_knolls(spatial_grid = fiji_grid)
```

### Deep-Sea Coral Habitat

```r
coral_habitat <- get_coral_habitat(spatial_grid = fiji_grid)
```

Returns habitat suitability for:

- **Antipatharia (Black Coral)**: Associated with increased biodiversity
- **Cold-water Corals**: Important habitats and nursery areas
- **Octocorals**: Important for invertebrates and fish

### Environmental Zones

Create data-driven bioregions based on environmental clustering:

```r
enviro_zones <- get_enviro_zones(
  spatial_grid = fiji_grid,
  num_clusters = 5,      # Number of zones
  show_plots = TRUE      # Show diagnostic plots
)
```

Environmental variables used include sea surface temperature, salinity, chlorophyll, nutrients, and more from Bio-ORACLE.

### Distance Calculations

```r
# Distance to coastline
dist_coast <- get_dist2coast(spatial_grid = fiji_grid)

# Distance to any polygon
dist_mpas <- get_dist2poly(
  spatial_grid = fiji_grid,
  polygon = my_mpas
)
```

### Combining Data

Combine all *oceandatr* outputs into a single dataset:

```r
# Download all layers
bathy <- get_bathymetry(fiji_grid, classify_bathymetry = TRUE)
geom <- get_geomorphology(fiji_grid) %>% remove_empty_layers()
seamounts <- get_seamounts(fiji_grid, buffer = 30000)
knolls <- get_knolls(fiji_grid)
corals <- get_coral_habitat(fiji_grid)
enviro <- get_enviro_zones(fiji_grid, num_clusters = 4)

# Convert grid to sf and combine
fiji_sf <- terra::as.polygons(fiji_grid) %>% 
  sf::st_as_sf() %>%
  dplyr::bind_cols(
    bathy %>% terra::values() %>% as.data.frame(),
    geom %>% terra::values() %>% as.data.frame(),
    seamounts %>% terra::values() %>% as.data.frame(),
    knolls %>% terra::values() %>% as.data.frame(),
    corals %>% terra::values() %>% as.data.frame(),
    enviro %>% terra::values() %>% as.data.frame()
  )
```

## spatialplanr {#sec-spatialplanr}

The *spatialplanr* package provides tools for streamlined spatial conservation prioritisation, with a focus on climate-smart planning.

### Installation

```r
remotes::install_github("SpatialPlanning/spatialplanr")
```

### Key Functions

#### Getting MPAs

Download marine protected areas from the World Database on Protected Areas:

```r
library(spatialplanr)

mpas <- splnr_get_MPAs(
  PlanUnits = fiji_sf,
  Countries = "Fiji"
)
```

#### Distance to Coast

Calculate distance from each planning unit to the coastline:

```r
fiji_sf <- fiji_sf %>%
  splnr_get_distCoast(custom_coast = fiji_land)
```

This adds a `coastDistance_km` column useful for cost layers.

#### Climate-Smart Features

*spatialplanr* provides functions for incorporating climate change:

```r
# Add climate projections to features
# (specific functions depend on your climate data source)
```

#### Applying Features to Grid

Transfer data from polygons or rasters to planning units:

```r
# From polygon data
habitat_in_grid <- splnr_apply_polygons(
  PUs = fiji_sf,
  data = habitat_polygons,
  field = "habitat_type"
)

# From raster data  
sst_in_grid <- splnr_apply_raster(
  PUs = fiji_sf,
  raster = sst_raster,
  method = "mean"
)
```

### Climate-Smart Planning Workflow

A typical climate-smart workflow with *spatialplanr*:

```r
library(spatialplanr)
library(prioritizr)

# 1. Prepare features with current and future distributions
features_current <- ...
features_future <- ...

# 2. Create climate-weighted features
# Higher weights for climate refugia

# 3. Build prioritizr problem incorporating climate
problem <- problem(PUs, features) %>%
  add_min_shortfall_objective(budget) %>%
  add_relative_targets(0.3) %>%
  # Add climate-smart constraints/weights
  ...

# 4. Solve
solution <- solve(problem)
```

## prioritizr {#sec-prioritizr}

The *prioritizr* package is the core optimisation engine for spatial conservation planning. *shinyplanr* uses *prioritizr* internally to solve planning problems.

### Installation

```r
# From CRAN
install.packages("prioritizr")

# Development version
install.packages(
  "prioritizr",
  repos = c(
    "https://prioritizr.r-universe.dev",
    "https://cloud.r-project.org"
  )
)
```

### Core Concepts

#### Building Problems

Every *prioritizr* analysis starts with a `problem()`:

```r
library(prioritizr)

# Create a problem
p <- problem(
  x = planning_units,    # sf or raster
  features = features    # Layer names or raster stack
)
```

#### Adding Objectives

Objectives define what you're trying to achieve:

```r
# Minimum set: smallest area meeting targets
p <- p %>% add_min_set_objective()

# Minimum shortfall: best outcome within budget
p <- p %>% add_min_shortfall_objective(budget = 1000)

# Maximum coverage: most representation within budget
p <- p %>% add_max_cover_objective(budget = 1000)
```

#### Setting Targets

Targets specify required representation:

```r
# Same target for all features
p <- p %>% add_relative_targets(0.3)  # 30% of each

# Different targets per feature
p <- p %>% add_relative_targets(c(0.2, 0.3, 0.5))

# Absolute targets
p <- p %>% add_absolute_targets(c(1000, 500, 2000))  # km²
```

#### Adding Constraints

Constraints restrict which solutions are valid:

```r
# Lock in existing protected areas
p <- p %>% add_locked_in_constraints(locked_in_layer)

# Lock out unavailable areas
p <- p %>% add_locked_out_constraints(locked_out_layer)

# Require contiguous selections
p <- p %>% add_contiguity_constraints()
```

#### Adding Penalties

Penalties discourage certain solution characteristics:

```r
# Boundary penalty (reduce fragmentation)
p <- p %>% add_boundary_penalties(penalty = 0.001)

# Linear penalties
p <- p %>% add_linear_penalties(penalty = 1, data = penalty_layer)
```

#### Selecting Solvers

Choose an optimisation solver:

```r
# Default: use best available
p <- p %>% add_default_solver()

# Specific solver
p <- p %>% add_gurobi_solver(gap = 0.01)
p <- p %>% add_highs_solver(gap = 0.01)
p <- p %>% add_cbc_solver()
```

#### Solving

Generate a solution:
  
```r
solution <- solve(p)
```

### Evaluating Solutions

After solving, evaluate the solution:

```r
# Number of selected planning units
eval_n_summary(p, solution)

# Total cost
eval_cost_summary(p, solution)

# Target achievement
eval_target_coverage_summary(p, solution)

# Feature representation
eval_feature_representation_summary(p, solution)
```

### Complete Example

```r
library(prioritizr)
library(sf)

# Load data
data(sim_pu_sf)        # Planning units
data(sim_features)     # Feature rasters

# Build problem
p <- problem(sim_pu_sf, sim_features, cost_column = "cost") %>%
  add_min_shortfall_objective(budget = 1000) %>%
  add_relative_targets(0.2) %>%
  add_locked_in_constraints("locked_in") %>%
  add_locked_out_constraints("locked_out") %>%
  add_boundary_penalties(penalty = 0.001) %>%
  add_default_solver(gap = 0.1)

# Print problem summary
print(p)

# Solve
solution <- solve(p)

# Evaluate
eval_target_coverage_summary(p, solution)

# Plot
plot(solution["solution_1"])
```

### Management Zones

For multiple-use planning, *prioritizr* supports management zones:

```r
# Define zones
zones <- zones(
  "no_take",        # Full protection
  "restricted_use", # Limited activities
  "general_use"     # Minimal restrictions
)

# Build zoned problem
p <- problem(
  x = planning_units,
  features = features,
  zones = zones
)

# Add zone-specific targets
p <- p %>% add_relative_targets(
  matrix(c(
    0.2, 0.1, 0,     # Feature 1 targets per zone
    0.3, 0.1, 0,     # Feature 2
    0.1, 0.2, 0      # Feature 3
  ), ncol = 3, byrow = TRUE)
)
```

## spatialgridr

The *spatialgridr* package (a dependency of *oceandatr*) provides grid creation and data extraction utilities.

### Installation

```r
remotes::install_github("emlab-ucsb/spatialgridr")
```

### Key Functions

#### Creating Grids

```r
library(spatialgridr)

grid <- get_grid(
  boundary = my_boundary,
  resolution = 10000,
  crs = my_projection,
  output = "sf_hex"  # or "sf_square", "terra"
)
```

#### Extracting Data to Grid

```r
# From polygon data
habitat_data <- get_data_in_grid(
  spatial_grid = grid,
  dat = habitat_polygons,
  feature_names = "habitat_type",
  meth = "average",       # or "binary", "sum"
  cutoff = 0.1            # Minimum proportion to include
)

# From raster data
raster_data <- get_data_in_grid(
  spatial_grid = grid,
  dat = my_raster,
  meth = "average"
)
```

## Package Versions and Compatibility

For consistent results, document package versions:

```r
# Check installed versions
packageVersion("prioritizr")
packageVersion("spatialplanr")
packageVersion("oceandatr")

# Session info
sessionInfo()
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Version Compatibility
If you encounter errors, ensure packages are up-to-date:

```r
remotes::install_github("SpatialPlanning/spatialplanr")
remotes::install_github("emlab-ucsb/oceandatr")
```
:::

## Summary

| Package | Use For |
|---------|---------|
| *oceandatr* | Downloading initial marine data layers |
| *spatialgridr* | Creating planning unit grids |
| *spatialplanr* | Climate-smart utilities, MPA data, helper functions |
| *prioritizr* | Building and solving optimisation problems |

These packages work together to support the full *shinyplanr* workflow from data acquisition to deployment.

## Further Resources

- [*prioritizr* tutorials](https://prioritizr.net/articles/)
- [*prioritizr* workshop materials](https://prioritizr.github.io/workshop/)
- [*oceandatr* vignettes](https://emlab-ucsb.github.io/oceandatr/articles/)
- [*spatialplanr* documentation](https://spatialplanning.github.io/spatialplanr/reference/)
